notes: Fey Faire · Shakespeare quote catalog for the RECITE combat action.  Each quote has: id, text (verbatim), play, act_scene, speaker (Shakespeare character), affinities (fey ids + court tags that RESPOND · a correct match doubles effect and often opens negotiation), anti_affinities (fey ids that MISQUOTE back), sp_cost, description.  Quotes are learned by: starting inventory (3), watching Big Top shows (1 per night, 6 nights = 6), buying paperbacks at the Bookstall (2 gold each, ~20 available across the run), and specific fey gifts on successful negotiation.  Player's quote memory is unlimited.  RECITE mechanically: if quote's affinities include the target's id or court, damage/effect is 2×; if anti_affinities include the target, the fey misquotes back and the protagonist takes minor Word damage.  Otherwise 1× or a no-op.

schema_notes.affinities: array of fey_ids (like 'titania', 'puck') and/or court tags (like 'seelie', 'unseelie', 'wildfey')
schema_notes.anti_affinities: array of the same shape · quote BACKFIRES against these
schema_notes.cost: SP cost to RECITE in combat

quotes.text: Lord, what fools these mortals be!
quotes.speaker: Puck
quotes.description: Puck laughs when you say this to him.  He always does.  It never gets old for him.

quotes.text: We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep.
quotes.speaker: Prospero
quotes.description: Prospero's own words.  Speaking them in the trailer where he wrote them has a specific effect.

quotes.text: I know a bank where the wild thyme blows, where oxlips and the nodding violet grows.
quotes.speaker: Oberon
quotes.description: Titania's location described in Oberon's words.  She recognizes the setting.

quotes.text: I'll be hang'd else.
quotes.speaker: Bottom
quotes.description: A Bottom line delivered as if by Bottom.  Puck approves of the impression.

quotes.text: The moon shines bright.  In such a night as this, when the sweet wind did gently kiss the trees...
quotes.speaker: Lorenzo
quotes.description: A night-verse that quiets a room.  Moth uses it to close her tent nightly.

quotes.text: Double, double, toil and trouble; fire burn and cauldron bubble.
quotes.speaker: The Three Witches
quotes.description: The witches' cauldron chant.  Speaking it to the weird sisters opens negotiation immediately.

quotes.text: By the pricking of my thumbs, something wicked this way comes.
quotes.speaker: Second Witch
quotes.description: A pre-attack warning.  Recited before an incoming fight, grants a preemptive strike.

quotes.text: Out, damned spot!  Out, I say!
quotes.speaker: Lady Macbeth
quotes.description: Guilt made verse.  Feys who hunt guilty men respond.

quotes.text: Fair is foul, and foul is fair.
quotes.speaker: The Three Witches
quotes.description: The witches' opening line.  Its ambiguity is its power.  Effective against any morally-flexible fey.

quotes.text: To be, or not to be, that is the question.
quotes.speaker: Hamlet
quotes.description: The famous soliloquy's opening.  Hamlet's Ghost takes a step forward at the sound.

quotes.text: There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio, than are dreamt of in your philosophy.
quotes.speaker: Hamlet
quotes.description: A concession to mystery.  Hamlet's Ghost approves of humility about the world's shape.

quotes.text: There's rosemary, that's for remembrance; pray, love, remember.
quotes.speaker: Ophelia
quotes.description: Ophelia's flower speech.  Speaking it in her presence is the ONLY way she'll respond directly.

quotes.text: Get thee to a nunnery.
quotes.speaker: Hamlet
quotes.description: Hamlet's cruel deflection.  Backfires HARD if Ophelia is present.

quotes.text: Frailty, thy name is woman!
quotes.speaker: Hamlet
quotes.description: Hamlet's misogyny.  Backfires against every named female fey · which is many.  Consider carefully.

quotes.text: All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players.
quotes.speaker: Jaques
quotes.description: The Faire IS a stage.  Recited at the Big Top, unlocks Cricket's deeper dialogue.

quotes.text: Journeys end in lovers meeting, every wise man's son doth know.
quotes.speaker: Feste
quotes.description: Feste's melancholic song.  Fools speak most truly.

quotes.text: Love moderately: long love doth so.
quotes.speaker: Friar Laurence
quotes.description: The Friar's warning.  Backfires against feys who want extremes.

quotes.text: O, then, I see Queen Mab hath been with you.  She is the fairies' midwife.
quotes.speaker: Mercutio
quotes.description: The one quote Queen Mab actually WANTS to hear.  Opens Mirror 6 (Dream) without sleeping if recited near the Sleep Tent.

quotes.text: Death lies on her, like an untimely frost upon the sweetest flower of all the field.
quotes.speaker: Capulet
quotes.description: Capulet mourning Juliet.  Powerful against death-adjacent feys; deeply insensitive to the flower retinue.

quotes.text: O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?
quotes.speaker: Juliet
quotes.description: Juliet's balcony.  Baobhan Sìth loves it.

quotes.text: He jests at scars that never felt a wound.
quotes.speaker: Romeo
quotes.description: Romeo answering Mercutio.  Speaking it near a scarred fey makes them consider you carefully.

quotes.text: This above all: to thine own self be true.
quotes.speaker: Polonius
quotes.description: Polonius's advice, later contradicted.  Speaking it to a fey seeking freedom (Ariel) is exceptionally effective.

quotes.text: O brave new world, that has such people in't!
quotes.speaker: Miranda
quotes.description: Miranda seeing humans for the first time.  Caliban does not appreciate the implication.

quotes.text: My way of life is fall'n into the sere, the yellow leaf.
quotes.speaker: Macbeth
quotes.description: Macbeth's autumn.  Green Man tilts his head slightly.

quotes.text: I have loved thee, Nurse.
quotes.speaker: Juliet
quotes.description: A young woman's love for her caretakers.  The Salt Sisters weep quietly.

quotes.text: This royal throne of kings, this scepter'd isle, this earth of majesty...
quotes.speaker: John of Gaunt
quotes.description: Gaunt's England.  Oberon nods; he likes a proud king.

quotes.text: Uneasy lies the head that wears a crown.
quotes.speaker: King Henry IV
quotes.description: The burden of rule.  Any monarch-fey responds.

quotes.text: I am that I am, and they that level at my abuses reckon up their own.
quotes.speaker: The Sonnets
quotes.description: A self-defense from the sonnets.  Feys who've been mistreated respond.

quotes.text: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?  Thou art more lovely and more temperate.
quotes.speaker: The Sonnets
quotes.description: The famous sonnet.  Universal Seelie flattery.

quotes.text: When my love swears that she is made of truth, I do believe her, though I know she lies.
quotes.speaker: The Sonnets
quotes.description: Loving despite knowing.  Melusine takes off her Saturday veil.

quotes.text: When shall we three meet again in thunder, lightning, or in rain?
quotes.speaker: The Three Witches
quotes.description: The witches' opening.  All three arrive at once if any is present.

quotes.text: There's not the smallest orb which thou behold'st but in his motion like an angel sings.
quotes.speaker: Lorenzo
quotes.description: The music of the spheres.  Music-fey resonate.

quotes.text: A horse!  A horse!  My kingdom for a horse!
quotes.speaker: Richard III
quotes.description: Richard offering the earth for transport.  Horse-feys sit up.

quotes.text: Now is the winter of our discontent made glorious summer by this sun of York.
quotes.speaker: Richard III
quotes.description: Winter turning to summer.  Winter-feys note the reversal.

quotes.text: This other Eden, demi-paradise, this fortress built by Nature for herself.
quotes.speaker: John of Gaunt
quotes.description: Nature's own fortress.  Wildfey elders nod.

quotes.text: One man in his time plays many parts, his acts being seven ages.
quotes.speaker: Jaques
quotes.description: Life in acts.  Ossory Wolf, halfway through his seven-year term, appreciates.

quotes.text: Ill met by moonlight, proud Titania.
quotes.speaker: Oberon
quotes.description: Oberon's opening.  Titania SHUNS you if you speak this to her.  Oberon smiles.

quotes.text: The lady doth protest too much, methinks.
quotes.speaker: Gertrude
quotes.description: Gertrude's cynicism.  Puck likes it; Ophelia does not.

quotes.text: The quality of mercy is not strained.  It droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven.
quotes.speaker: Portia
quotes.description: Mercy as rain.  Cruel feys hate it; kind ones weep.

quotes.text: This thing of darkness I acknowledge mine.
quotes.speaker: Prospero
quotes.description: Prospero's line to Caliban at the end.  Caliban weeps.  Recruits him immediately if all other conditions met.

quotes.text: Flout 'em and scout 'em, and scout 'em and flout 'em; thought is free.
quotes.speaker: Stephano
quotes.description: Drunks and drunks.  Cluricaune raises his cup.

quotes.text: Be not afeard: the isle is full of noises, sounds, and sweet airs that give delight and hurt not.
quotes.speaker: Caliban
quotes.description: Caliban's gentler moment.  Ariel and Caliban both pause when it's spoken.

quotes.text: Full fathom five thy father lies; of his bones are coral made.
quotes.speaker: Ariel
quotes.description: Ariel's sea-song.  Every sea-fey listens.

quotes.text: Out, out, brief candle!  Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player.
quotes.speaker: Macbeth
quotes.description: Macbeth's despair.  Moth's candle extinguishes for a moment when this is spoken.

quotes.text: When came you to speak with him?  When did you last see your father?
quotes.speaker: not-Shakespeare · a Yeats fragment sneaking in
quotes.description: Not Shakespeare · a Yeats-echoed line Rocha snuck into the game.  Death-messenger feys respond.

quotes.text: All hail, Macbeth, that shalt be king hereafter!
quotes.speaker: Third Witch
quotes.description: The Third Witch's prophecy.  Speaking it fills Hecate's cauldron with something.

quotes.text: Come, let's away to prison: we two alone will sing like birds i' the cage.
quotes.speaker: Lear
quotes.description: Lear's last hopeful moment.  The Salt Sisters recognize their King (they played him in the show).

quotes.text: Nothing will come of nothing.
quotes.speaker: Lear
quotes.description: Lear's tetchy demand.  Small feys who feel unappreciated respond.

quotes.text: The air, a chartered libertine, is still.
quotes.speaker: Canterbury
quotes.description: The still air.  Air-feys pause and let it settle over them.

quotes.text: By yea and no, I do believe your fair cousin is wronged.
quotes.speaker: Slender
quotes.description: A Merry-Wives line.  Herne prefers his own play.

quotes.text: I will bandy with thee in faction, I will o'er-run thee with policy.
quotes.speaker: Touchstone
quotes.description: Touchstone's rhetorical joust.  Trickster-feys love a good verbal fencing match.
